coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescent probe based on sulfur quantum dots and calcium ion for sensitive and visual detection of doxycycline in food
coredata.dc:creator: Zhuang, Yuerui
Lin, Bixia
Yu, Ying
Wang, Yumin
Zhang, Li
Cao, Yujuan
Guo, Manli
coredata.dc:description: The residue of doxycycline in food can cause harm to human. Therefore, the detection of doxycycline residue is necessary. Herein, a ratiometric fluorescent probe was designed based on sulfur quantum dots (S dots) and Ca2+. Due to static quenching and inter filter effect between doxycycline and S dots, doxycycline quenched fluorescence of S dots at 450 nm. Meanwhile, doxycycline and Ca2+ formed fluorescent complex through coordination to produce new peak at 520 nm. The ratio of fluorescence intensity (F520/F450) and doxycycline concentration showed good linear relationship with detection limit of 0.19 μM. The fluorescence color of S dots/Ca2+ changed from blue to light green with increasing doxycycline concentration, which was applied for visual semi-quantitative detection of doxycycline. Moreover, the method was used for detecting doxycycline in milk and fish samples with recoveries in the range of 91%-110%. The method showed good application potential in detection of doxycycline in food samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfur quantum dots
Calcium ion
Doxycycline
Ratiometric fluorescence
Visual semi-quantitation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction of ratiometric fluorescent probe S dots/Ca2+
Calibration curve and selectivity for doxycycline
Synthesis and characterization of S dots
Detection mechanism of doxycycline by S dots/Ca2+
Calibration curve and selectivity of doxycycline

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of S dots. (A) TEM image; (B) UV–Vis spectrum; (C) Fluorescence spectra under various excitation wavelengths; (D) Excitation and emission spectra before (curve 1 and 2) and after (curve 3 and 4) treating with hydrogen peroxide.

Feasibility of S dots/Ca2+ probe for doxycycline detection. (A) Fluorescence spectra of doxycycline (curve 1), doxycycline + Ca2+ (curve 2), S dots (curve 3), S dots + Ca2+ (curve 4), S dots + doxycycline (curve 5), S dots + Ca2+ + doxycycline (curve 6). The concentration of doxycycline and Ca2+ is 100 μM; (B) Fluorescence spectra of S dots/Ca2+ probe with various concentrations of doxycycline.

Quenching mechanism of S dots by doxycycline. (A) UV–Vis spectra of S dots (1), doxycycline (2), S dots + doxycycline (3); (B) Zeta potentials; (C) Superposition of the excitation, emission spectra of S dots and the absorption spectrum of doxycycline; (D) The Stern-Volmer plot between S dots and doxycyline.

Coordination of calcium ion and doxycycline. (A) UV–Vis spectra of doxycycline with different concentrations of Ca2+; (B) UV–Vis spectra of doxycycline with different metal ions (150 μM); (C) Fluorescence spectra of doxycycline with different metal ions (150 μM); (D) The effect of coordination ratio of Ca2+ and doxycycline (150 μM) on the fluorescence intensity.

Calibration curve and selectivity. (A) Linear relationship between F520/F450 and doxycycline concentration detected by S dots/Ca2+; (B) Linear fitting diagram in the concentration range of 0.5–25 μM; (C) Interference of ions (100 μM); (D) Interference of analogues (100 μM).
The fluorescence images of (A) S dots/Ca2+ and (B) Single S dots with different concentrations of doxycycline under UV irradiation.
Schematic illustration of the detection principle for doxycycline by a ratiometric fluorescent probe based on S dots and Ca2+.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescent probe based on sulfur quantum dots and calcium ion for sensitive and visual detection of doxycycline in food

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biomedicine, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biomedicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry, Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biomedicine, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: No. 378 Waihuan West Road, University City, Guangzhou, Guangdong 510006, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The residue of doxycycline in food can cause harm to human. Therefore, the detection of doxycycline residue is necessary. Herein, a ratiometric fluorescent probe was designed based on sulfur quantum dots (S dots) and Ca2+. Due to static quenching and inter filter effect between doxycycline and S dots, doxycycline quenched fluorescence of S dots at 450 nm. Meanwhile, doxycycline and Ca2+ formed fluorescent complex through coordination to produce new peak at 520 nm. The ratio of fluorescence intensity (F520/F450) and doxycycline concentration showed good linear relationship with detection limit of 0.19 μM. The fluorescence color of S dots/Ca2+ changed from blue to light green with increasing doxycycline concentration, which was applied for visual semi-quantitative detection of doxycycline. Moreover, the method was used for detecting doxycycline in milk and fish samples with recoveries in the range of 91%-110%. The method showed good application potential in detection of doxycycline in food samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Doxycycline (Dox) is a tetracycline antibiotic composed of β-diketoester group, and often used as an additive in animal feed (Ghaemi & Absalan, 2015; Li et al., 2014). Excessive use of doxycycline during animal production may result in the presence of its residue in food. When doxycycline remaining in food is ingested by human, it can cause liver damage, allergic reaction, and gastrointestinal diseases, thereby posing a hazard to human health (Abdulsattar, Hadi, Richardson, Iles, & Pamme, 2020). In order to ensure the food safety, the European Union has declared the maximum residue limit (MRL) of doxycycline in fowl with 100 µg/kg in muscle, 300 µg/kg in skin, fat and liver, and 600 µg/kg in kidney.

At present, a variety of technologies for detecting doxycycline have been developed, such as electrochemistry (Ali, Mohamed, El-Sonbati, Diab, & Elkfass, 2018; Cui, Guo, Yan, Li, & He, 2020; García-Campaña, Gámiz-Gracia, Lara, Del Olmo Iruela, & Cruces-Blanco, 2009; Mu, Liu, Xu, Tian, & Luan, 2012), liquid chromatography (Da Silveira, Oliveira, Almeida, Hoff, & Mallmann, 2018; Dil, Ghaedi, Asfaram, Tayebi, & Mehrabi, 2020), flow injection analysis (Palamy & Ruengsitagoon, 2017), thin layer chromatography (Mohammad & Zawilla, 2009), immunoassay (Ashuo et al., 2020; Le, Yi, Wei, & Liu, 2015; Le, Zhao, Wei, & Bi, 2012), etc. However, most of these methods require complicated sample pretreatment, and the operation process is tedious and time-consuming. It is necessary to develop a simple and efficient method to detect doxycycline. Due to the advantages of high sensitivity, good selectivity and simplicity, fluorescence analysis has been used for doxycycline detection. For example, Song et al. (2017) reported the preparation of sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (S, N-CD). Doxycycline quenched the fluorescence of S, N-CD through static quenching, thus the detection of doxycycline was achieved. Feng et al. (2018) prepared nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots integrating molecularly imprinted polymers (NCQD@MIP) to detect doxycycline. After NCQD@MIP interacted with doxycycline, the fluorescence was quenched.

Recently, S dots, a new kind of fluorescent nanomaterial, have received much attention from scientists. Li et al. (2015) prepared S dots by using HNO3 to oxidize S2- in CdS quantum dots. Shen et al. (2018) and Xiao et al. (2019) prepared S dots by top-down assembly fission method and one-step hydrothermal method, respectively. Song et al. (2020) proposed the oxidization of polysulfides to zero-valent sulfur by O2, thereby preparing S dots. With the development of preparation methods, S dots have been applied in various fields, especially in detection of ions. For example, Wang et al. (2019) used cobalt ion to aggregate S dots and quench their fluorescence. When norfloxacin was present, norfloxacin coordinated with cobalt ion to restore the fluorescence. Thus, the detection of cobalt ion and norfloxacin was achieved. Qiao et al. (2020) reported that mercury ion combined with S dots based on the S-Hg-S bond to reduce the fluorescence. Then, S dots were applied to monitor the distribution of mercury ion in cells. Fu et al. (2020) used S dots to detect iron ion. Because iron ion had a high affinity with the oxygen atom of S dots, the fluorescence of S dots was quenched. Chen et al. (2016) reported that silver ion caused the aggregation induced emission of S dots. The enhanced fluorescence was applied to detect silver ion. Until now, S dots are mainly focused on the detection of metal ions, and rarely used for the detection of other substances such as organic molecules.

Studies show that some metal ions can coordinate with non-fluorescent or weak fluorescent organic molecules to form strong fluorescent complex. For example, Xia et al. (2012) used aluminum ion to coordinate with norfloxacin to produce strong fluorescence, thereby establishing an “turn-on” fluorescence method for detecting norfloxacin. Similarly, El-Kommos et al. (2003) used quinolone antibiotics to coordinate with metal ions such as zirconium, molybdenum, vanadium and tungsten to produce fluorescent complexes for detecting quinolone antibiotics. In such type of detection system, when another fluorescent material is introduced, the fluorescent material can be used as a reference to construct a ratiometric fluorescent probe with metal ion for detecting the organic molecule. Thereby, the accuracy and anti-interference of the detection method can be improved. Moreover, it is reported that doxycycline can coordinate with calcium ion through intramolecular oxygen of ketone and hydroxyl groups to form a stable six-membered ring complex (Hussein, Walker, Peralta-Inga, & Murray, 2001), which can weaken the intramolecule vibration of doxycycline and increase the radiation energy (Leng, Zhao, Wang, & Li, 2013). Inspired by the above-mentioned literature, herein, a ratiometric fluorescent probe based on S dots/Ca2+ is designed for detecting doxycycline. The principle is shown in Scheme 1 .

Doxycycline (>99%), kanamycin (>94%) and luteolin (>98%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Amoxicillin (>99%) was purchased from Bomei Biotech Co. Ltd (Hefei, China). Roxithromycin (>95%) and thiamphenicol (>98%) were purchased from Meilun Biotech Co. Ltd (Dalian, China). Metronidazole (>99%) was purchased from Macklin Biotech Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Norfloxacin (>98%) was purchased from Shenggong Biotech Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Sublimed sulfur and polyethylene glycol (PEG-400) were purchased from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Hydrogen peroxide, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, and disodium hydrogen phosphate were purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory (Guangzhou, China). Sodium hydroxide was purchased from Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Tianjin, China). The reagents used were all analytically pure unless otherwise specified. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water that was obtained from a Millipore Milli-Q water purification system (Billerica, MA, FRA). Tilapia was chosen as the fish sample. Both tilapia and milk were purchased from the Beiting Market (Guangzhou, China).

The fluorescence spectra were recorded by a fluorescence spectrometer (F-4600, Hitach, Tokyo, Japan), and the excitation voltage was 400 V. The UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2700, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). Zeta potential was measured by a Zetasizer nano-particle potential analyzer (ZS90, Malvern, Worchester, UK). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were collected by a transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100HR, JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). For TEM analysis, S dots solution was dropped onto copper grid and dried at ambient temperature. The high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) analysis was performed on a liquid chromatograph (LC-20A, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). The chromatograph column was kromasil 100-5C18 and the mobile phase was oxalic acid /acetonitrile/methanol. The flow rate, the column temperature and the detection wavelength were set as 1.0 mL/min, 40 ℃, and 350 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of ratiometric fluorescent probe S dots/Ca2+
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of S dots was referred to literature (Shen et al., 2018) with some modification. Firstly, 1.4 g of sublimed sulfur, 4 g of sodium hydroxide, 3 mL of PEG-400 and 50 mL of ultrapure water were mixed. The mixture was refluxed for 72 h under continuously stirring at 70 ℃ to generate S dots. Then, 3.75% hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was added into S dots solution with a volume ratio of 4:3 (H2O2 : S dots), and the color of S dots changed from dark yellow to light yellow. Subsequently, the S dots solution treated with hydrogen peroxide was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was stored at 4 °C for further use. In order to obtain the ratiometric fluorescent probe S dots/Ca2+, S dots solution and 1 mM of calcium ion solution were mixed uniformly with a volume ratio of 1:1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration curve and selectivity for doxycycline
ce:section.ce:para: In a series of colorimetric tubes, 160 μL of S dots/Ca2+ probe solution, 140 μL of phosphate buffer solution (PB,10 mM, pH = 6.0) and 1 mM of doxycycline standard solution with different volumes were added. Sequentially, the mixture was diluted to a final volume of 400 μL with ultrapure water, and it was allowed to incubate at room temperature for 5 min in the dark. Then, the fluorescence spectrum of each mixture was measured at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm and a PMT voltage of 400 V. The ratio of the fluorescence intensities at 520 nm and 450 nm was used as the ordinate, and the concentration of doxycycline was used as the abscissa to establish the calibration curve for doxycycline detected with S dots/Ca2+. Meanwhile, the calibration curve for doxycycline detection with single S dots was also performed as a control.

In order to evaluate the selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescence method based on S dots/Ca2+, the effects of common coexisting ions and analogues on the detection were investigated. The coexisting ions included CO3 2–, SO4 2-, NO3 –, Cl-, Na+, K+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Fe3+, the analogues included amoxicillin, thiamphenicol, metronidazole, roxithromycin, luteolin, norfloxacin, kanamycin, etc.

For the visual semi-quantitative detection, 160 μL of S dots/Ca2+ probe solution, 140 μL of PB buffer solution (10 mM, pH = 6), and a series of 1 mM doxycycline standard solutions with different volumes were added and diluted to a final volume of 400 μL with ultrapure water. The mixture was incubated for 5 min, and then observed under 365 nm ultraviolet light. As the control group, 60 μL of the single S dots solution was also performed as S dots/Ca2+.

The preparation of milk and fish samples was performed as follows. 4 mL of 1% (v/v) trichloroacetic acid was added to 5 mL of milk, and ultrasonically mixed for 20 min to precipitate the protein in the milk. Then, the mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, the supernatant was filtered through 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove protein, and diluted to a final volume of 100 mL. To obtain the fish sample, 5 mL of PB solution (pH = 6.0) was added to 1 g of crushed fish, ultrasonically mixed for 20 min, and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min. Then, 5 mL of 1% (v/v) trichloroacetic acid was added to the supernatant and ultrasonically mixed for 20 min to precipitate the protein, centrifuged again and filtered with 0.22 μm filter membrane. The solution was diluted to a final volume of 20 mL. Both the milk and fish samples were stored at 4 °C for subsequent use.

The level of doxycycline in the milk and fish samples were determined according to the same method for calibration curve. Subsequently, the recovery experiments were carried out with standard addition method. The spiked concentrations of doxycycline were 1, 5 and 20 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of S dots
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain S dots with high fluorescence intensity, the influences of different synthesis conditions on the fluorescence intensity of S dots are studied. The results are shown in Fig. S1. With the increase of the reaction time, the fluorescence intensity of S dots increases firstly and then decreased. When the reaction time is 72 h, the strongest fluorescence intensity is obtained. Similarly, the strongest fluorescence intensity is achieved when the amount of PEG-400 is 3 mL. The fluorescence intensity of S dots increases remarkably at first and reaches the platform when the concentration of hydrogen peroxide is 3.75%. Therefore, the reaction time of 72 h, 3 mL of PEG-400, and 3.75% hydrogen peroxide were chosen as the optimal synthesis conditions for S dots.

S dots are characterized by transmission electron microscopy, UV–Vis spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The TEM image is shown in Fig. 1 A. S dots have good dispersibility and uniform size distribution of about 3.5 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image shows S dots have clear lattice fringes with lattice size of 0.23 nm, which is consistent with that reported in the literature (Shen et al., 2018). In Fig. 1B, S dots have an absorption peak at 260 nm and shows bright blue fluorescence under UV irradiation of 365 nm. In Fig. 1C, the emission spectra of S dots after treatment with hydrogen peroxide are obtained under the excitation wavelength range of 320–400 nm. With the excitation wavelength increases from 320 to 400 nm, the fluorescence peak shows a slight red shift. When the excitation wavelength is 370 nm, the fluorescence intensity reaches the strongest at 450 nm. As shown in Fig. 1D, S dots before treating with hydrogen peroxide have the strongest emission peak at 450 nm under excitation at 404 nm. By comparison, after the treatment with hydrogen peroxide, the optimal excitation wavelength of S dots is blue shifted. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity is significantly increased by about 11.5 times, which indicates that the treatment of hydrogen peroxide decreases the particle size of S dots, reduces the surface defects, and thus enhances the fluorescence intensity. By using quinine sulfate as the reference standard, the quantum yield of S dots is measured as 34.1% which is higher than that reported in the literature (Shen et al., 2018). The anti-photobleaching performance of S dots is explored in Fig. S2A. When S dots are continuously irradiated under 370 nm excitation for 1 h, the fluorescence intensity remains above 90% of the origin fluorescence intensity, which proves that S dots have good photobleaching resistance. In Fig. S2B, S dots still have good dispersion after storing at 4 ℃ in the dark for 24 days, and the fluorescence intensity remains above 95%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection mechanism of doxycycline by S dots/Ca2+
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 2 A, single doxycycline exhibits no fluorescence signal (curve 1), but the addition of calcium ion produces a strong fluorescence peak at 520 nm (curve 2). The fluorescence of S dots remains the same before and after the addition of calcium ion (curve 3 and 4). However, when doxycycline is present, the fluorescence intensity of S dots at 450 nm is quenched (curve 5). The quenching degree gradually enhances with the increase of the doxycycline concentration (Fig. S3). When doxycycline is added to the mixture of S dots and calcium ion, the fluorescence intensity of S dots at 450 nm decreases and a new fluorescence peak appears at 520 nm (curve 6). As the concentration of doxycycline increases, the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm gradually decreases, and the intensity of the new fluorescence peak at 520 nm gradually increases (Fig. 2B). These results clearly manifests that S dots and calcium ion are able to be used to construct a ratiometric fluorescent probe for the detection of doxycycline.

The detection mechanism of doxycycline by S dots/Ca2+ probe is explored by using UV–Vis spectroscopy, zeta potential analysis, fluorescence spectrum. Firstly, the quenching mechanism of S dots by doxycycline is discussed. The absorption spectra of S dots, doxycycline, and the mixture are showed in Fig. 3 A. After the addition of S dots, the absorption peak of doxycycline has a significant shift, which proves that the complex is formed between S dots and doxycycline. In Fig. 3B, Zeta potential is used to further explore the quenching mechanism of S dots by doxycycline. S dots are negatively charged, and doxycycline are positively charged. However, the mixture has a more positive potential relative to the single S dots, indicating that S dots and doxycycline have electrostatic interaction to form a complex. The electrostatic interaction is attributed to the abundant sulfite and sulfonic groups on the surface of S dots, and the multiple amino groups in the doxycycline molecule. Fig. 3C is the overlap diagram of the excitation and emission spectra of S dots, and the absorption spectrum of doxycycline. The excitation spectrum of S dots has an evident overlap with the absorption spectrum of doxycycline, which indicates doxycycline has inter filter effect on S dots. Moreover, the Stern-Volmer plot between S dots and doxycyline is shown in Fig. 3D. The result presents the constant KSV decreases with the increase of temperature, indicating that the quenching of S dots by doxycycline is static quenching. In summary, the quenching of doxycycline on S dots is mainly due to the electrostatic interaction between doxycycline and S dots to form a complex, and an inter filter effect between doxycycline and S dots. The synergistic effect of static quenching and inter filter effect causes doxycycline to sensitively quench the fluorescence of S dots.

Subsequently, the effect between calcium ion and doxycycline is discussed. Fig. 4 A shows that the absorption peak of doxycycline has a significant red shift with the concentration increase of calcium ion, indicating that doxycycline interacts with calcium ion to form complex. Because doxycycline is able to coordinate with calcium ion through the intramolecular oxygen atom to form a stable six-membered ring complex (Hussein et al., 2001), the conformational rotation of the doxycycline molecule is restricted, the rigid plane is increased, and the radiation energy is enhanced (Leng et al., 2013). Therefore, the addition of calcium ion causes non-fluorescent doxycycline to produce a strong fluorescence peak at 520 nm (curve 2 in Fig. 2A). Due to the similar arrangement of the extranuclear electrons, magnesium ion and aluminum ion may also coordinate with doxycycline as calcium ion. Therefore, the coordination effects of calcium ion, magnesium ion and aluminum ion with doxycycline are compared. In Fig. 4B, when calcium ion, magnesium ion or aluminum ion with the same concentration is added to doxycycline, the absorbance is enhanced, and the absorption wavelength red shifts. But the red shift degree of the absorption wavelength caused by calcium ion is the largest. Fig. 4C shows that when the same concentration of calcium ion, magnesium ion or aluminum ion is added to doxycycline, a new fluorescence peak around 520 nm is generated. The fluorescence intensity caused by Ca2+ is the strongest, which is about 3 times and 5 times to those caused by Mg2+ and Al3+, respectively. This further proves the coordination between calcium ion and doxycycline, also indicates the use of calcium ion has better sensitivity than other metal ions to construct the ratiometric fluorescent probe for detecting doxycycline. The coordination ratio of calcium ion and doxycycline is explored in Fig. 4D. When the ratio of the amount of calcium ion to doxycycline reaches 2:1, the fluorescence intensity tends to stabilize. This indicates that the coordination ratio of calcium ion to doxycycline is 2:1. Thus, the possible structure of the complex formed by calcium ion and doxycycline is deduced in Fig. S4. In Fig. S5, the absorbance changes little when the ratio of the amount of calcium ion to doxycycline exceeds 2:1. This also proves that the coordination ratio of calcium ion to doxycycline is 2:1, which is consistent with the fluorescence results.

In summary, the detection mechanism of doxycycline by S dots/Ca2+ includes two parts: (1) S dots and doxycycline form complex through electrostatic interaction to quench the fluorescence of S dots. Meanwhile, doxycycline has an inter filter effect on S dots, thereby further quenching the fluorescence of S dots at 450 nm. (2) Calcium ion and doxycycline form complex through coordination, which increases the rigid plane of doxycycline. Thus, the fluorescence is enhanced to produce a new fluorescence peak at 520 nm. Therefore, doxycycline combines with both S dots and calcium ion to form complex, which is also proved by UV–Vis spectra in Fig. S6. The absorption peak from the mixture of S dots/Ca2+ and doxycycline shifts by comparing with those of single S dots or single calcium ion mixing with doxycycline.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration curve and selectivity of doxycycline
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the fluorescence change of S dots/Ca2+ caused by doxycycline, a ratiometric fluorescence method for detecting doxycycline is established. In order to achieve the optimal detection of doxycycline, pH of the buffer solution, reaction time, dosage of S dots, and concentration of calcium ion are optimized. As shown in Fig. S7A, the fluorescence intensity ratio of 520 nm and 450 nm reaches the maximum at the pH of 6. In order to fit with the pH range of milk and fish meat, a pH of 6 is chosen for subsequent analysis. In Fig. S7B, the fluorescence intensity ratio of 520 nm and 450 nm stabilizes when the reaction time reaches 5 min. Therefore, 5 min is selected as the optimal reaction time of S dots/Ca2+ and doxycycline. In Fig. S7C and S7D, the fluorescence intensity reaches the maximum when 80 μL of S dots and 200 μM of calcium ion are used. Thus, the optimal dosage of S dots is 80 μL and the optimal concentration of calcium ion is 200 μM. In addition, the detection conditions for doxycycline by the single S dots are also optimized in Fig. S8. The results shows that the optimal dosage of S dots is 60 μL, the optimal pH of the buffer solution is 6.0, the reaction temperature is room temperature, and the reaction time is 5 min.

Under the optimal detection conditions, the calibration curve of doxycycline detected by S dots/Ca2+ is explored in Fig. 5 A and 5B. In the concentration range of 0–250 μM, the fluorescence intensity ratio of 520 nm and 450 nm gradually enhances with the increase of doxycycline concentration. The F520/F450 value and the concentration of doxycycline present a two-stage linear relationship. The linear equation in the range of 0.5–25 μM is F520/F450 = 0.006310 CDox + 0.3327 (CDox is the concentration of doxycycline, μM). The correlation coefficient R2 is 0.9880, and the detection limit is estimated to be 0.19 μM (LOD = 3σ/S, σ is the standard deviation of 11 blank samples, and S is the slope of the calibration curve). The linear equation in the range of 25–250 μM is F520/F450 = -3.569 log CDox + 4.515 with correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9966. The calibration curve for detecting doxycycline by single S dots is also investigated in Fig. S9. In the concentration range of 0–250 μM, the fluorescence quenching rate gradually enhances with the increase of doxycycline concentration. In the concentration range of 5–125 μM, the fluorescence quenching rate has a good linear relationship with the concentration of doxycycline. The linear equation is (F0-F)/F0 = 0.006520 CDox + 0.05185 with the correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9829, and the detection limit of 1.2 μM (LOD = 3σ/S). By comparison, it is obvious that the addition of calcium ion produces the lower detection limit and improves the detection sensitivity of doxycycline.

The selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescence method is investigated. As shown in Fig. 5C and 5D, the fluorescence intensity ratio has little change with the addition of the interfering substances (blue column). Only the addition of doxycycline causes the major increase of the fluorescence intensity ratio. Moreover, the presence of the interfering substances has no significant effect on the determination of doxycycline (red column). The above results show the ratiometric fluorescence method has good selectivity for doxycycline.

Fig. 6 presents the images of S dots/Ca2+ and single S dots with different concentrations of doxycycline under ultraviolet irradiation of 365 nm. Fig. 6A shows that as the concentration of doxycycline increases, the fluorescence color of S dots/Ca2+ gradually changes from blue to light green. When the concentration of doxycycline is 20 μM, the color starts to change obviously. Thus, 20 μM is chosen as the control concentration for the visual semi-quantitative detection of doxycycline. For example, when the fluorescence of the sample is blue, the concentration of doxycycline is less than 20 μM. When the fluorescence of the sample is light green, the concentration of doxycycline is >20 μM. For the single S dots, Fig. 6B shows that as the concentration of doxycycline increases, the blue fluorescence of S dots only gradually decreases. By contrast, the fluorescence color change produced by S dots/Ca2+ is more obvious. By using the fluorescence color change from S dots/Ca2+, the visual semi-quantitative detection of doxycycline can be achieved. The operation is simple without the need of professional equipment.

In order to evaluate the feasibility of the ratiometric fluorescence method for detecting doxycycline in samples, the milk and fish samples are tested by standard addition method, and then the recovery ratios are calculated. As presented in Table 1 , the recoveries of the spiked milk are in the range from 91% to 110% with RSD (n = 3) in the range of 0.3%-4.4%. The recoveries of spiked fish are in the range from 91.2% to 104% with RSD (n = 3) of 0.2%-1.6%. In addition, in order to further verify the accuracy of the method, high performance liquid chromatography is used to detect doxycycline in these samples (Lai et al., 2019). The results measured by the ratiometric fluorescence method are consistent with those obtained by HPLC, which proves that the ratiometric fluorescence method has good accuracy.

The ratiometric fluorescence method based on S dots/Ca2+ is compared with other reported detection methods for doxycycline such as fluorescence method (Fu et al., 2018; Su, Wei, Mao, & Yang, 2018; Yang, Wang, Lu, & Yang, 2018), UV–Vis spectrophotometry (Lorenzetti, Domin, & Lista, 2017; Palamy & Wirat, 2017; Siddiqui, Anwar, Ahmed, Naqvi, Ahah, & Ahmed, 2020; Song, Qiao, Liu, & Qi, 2020; Xu, Ma, Chen, Yu, Sun, & Zhang, 2020), and electrochemical method (Ali et al., 2018). The results in Table S1 show that the sensitivity of this method is higher than those of UV–Vis spectrophotometry. In addition, compared with electrochemical method and other fluorescence methods, this method shows the equivalent sensitivity, but this method has simple operation process and the practicability for visual semi-quantitative detection.

Based on the quenching effect of doxycycline on the fluorescence of S dots and the coordination with calcium ion to form strong fluorescent complex, a ratiometric fluorescent probe is designed for the doxycycline detection by using S dots and calcium ion. Under the optimal experimental conditions, the detection of doxycycline shows two linear relationships in the range of 0.5–25 and 25–250 μM with the detection limit of 0.19 μM. The method is applied for the detection of doxycycline in milk and fish samples, and the results are consistent with those obtained by high performance liquid chromatography. In addition, the method is used for visual semi-quantitative detection of doxycycline. The operation is simple and fast with no professional equipment, which has good application potential in the on-site detection of the doxycycline residue in food.

Yuerui Zhuang: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Bixia Lin: Data curation, Conceptualization, Project administration, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision. Ying Yu: Conceptualization, Project administration, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision. Yumin Wang: Resources, Writing - review & editing. Li Zhang: Resources, Writing - review & editing. Yujuan Cao: Resources. Manli Guo: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21575043, 22004039); Fund of Department of Education of Guangdong Province (No. 2020KTSCX033); Ministry of Education University-Industry Collaborative Education Program (No. 201901100003); Platform Con-struction Project of Guangzhou Science Technology and Innovation Commission (No. 15180001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2021.129720.

ce:section.ce:para:
